agi.li.investmentsTT8: You are reporting from Form 8814 (relating to election to report child's interest and dividends).
agi.li.investmentsTT9: You have income or loss from a passive activity.
agi.li.movingTT1: Direct travel from your old home to your new one
agi.li.movingTT2: Cost of packing and moving your personal belongings
agi.li.pensionTT1: You didn't contribute anything or aren't considered to have contributed anything for your pension or annuity
agi.li.pensionTT2: Your employer didn't withhold contributions from your salary, or
agi.li.pensionTT3: You received all your contributions tax-free in prior years

agi.li.studentLoanTT1: For you, your spouse, or a person who was your dependent when you took out the loan;
agi.li.studentLoanTT2: Paid or incurred within a reasonable period of time before or after you took out the loan; and

agi.li.taxDeferredRetirementTT2: Section 457 plans, but not Roth plans

agi.p.agiDisqualifier1: Based on the amounts you entered, you can't claim the earned income credit because your AGI is over the limit for your filing status and number of qualifying children.
agi.p.agiDisqualifier2: :note For more information about the income limits for the Earned Income Credit, see :tablesLink.
agi.a.agiDisqualifier: Earned Income and Tax Credit Tables
agi.p.alimonyTT1: If you made alimony or separate maintenance payments under a court order that was executed before January 1, 2019  and the order wasn't modified to specifically state that you can't deduct your payments, enter the amount you paid during the year here.
agi.p.alimonyTT2: Don't include amounts you paid for:
agi.p.alimonyTT3: Also don't include amounts if the court order was modified and states that you can't deduct your payments.
agi.p.alimonyTT4: Alimony or separate maintenance made under a court order executed after December 31, :year
agi.p.alimonyTT6: If you made alimony or separate maintenance payments under a court order that was executed before January 1, 2019 and the order didn't specifically state that you can't deduct your payments, enter the amount you paid during year here.
agi.p.businessTT2: You may include expenses that are common and accepted in your field of trade, business, or profession, or expenses for things that are helpful and appropriate for your business.
agi.p.businessTT3: If you don't fit into one of the listed categories, you may not claim adjustments for these expenses.
agi.p.businessTT4: For more information and a list of qualified expenses, see :form2106.
agi.p.cafeteriaTT1: A "cafeteria plan" is a collection of benefits offered by your employer where you can choose what you want to participate in.
agi.p.cafeteriaTT2: Enter any pre-tax contributions (payroll deductions) from any of the following:
agi.p.disabilityRetirementTT1: If you retired on disability, we consider the taxable benefits you received under your employer's disability retirement plan earned income for the purposes of the EITC until you reach minimum retirement age. The minimum retirement age is the earliest age at which you could have received a pension or annuity if you weren’t disabled.
agi.p.disabilityRetirementTT2: Include temporary or short-term disability in your wages.
agi.p.earlyWithdrawalPenaltyTT: If you withdraw funds from a certificate of deposit or other deferred interest account before maturity, you may be charged a penalty. You can find this penalty on your Form 1099-INT in :box2. Enter that amount here.
agi.p.earnedIncomeDisqualifier1: Based on the amounts you entered, you can't claim the earned income credit because your earned income is over the limit for your filing status and number of qualifying children.
agi.p.earnedIncomeDisqualifier2: :note For more information about the income limits for the Earned Income Credit, see :tablesLink.
agi.a.earnedIncomeDisqualifier: Earned Income and Tax Credit Tables
agi.p.educatorTT1: If you're a teacher, enter up to $300 ($600 if married filing jointly and both you and your spouse are teachers) of unreimbursed expenses such as:
agi.p.federalIncomeNotWithheldNote: Your self employment tax is being correctly calculated and applied to your Adjusted Gross Income (AGI).
agi.p.federalIncomeNotWithheldTT1: Report income earned from gig work, even if it's a side job, part time or temporary.
agi.p.federalIncomeNotWithheldTT2: Enter income here if you earn money from a job even if income taxes were not withheld. You may or may not have received a Form 1099 for all income.
agi.p.federalIncomeNotWithheldTT3: The income may have been earned through an app or website to:
agi.p.federalIncomeNotWithheldTT4: Your gross income includes all the income you received. We will subtract your expenses later.
agi.p.foreignEarnedIncomeTT1: Foreign earned income is income earned while living and working outside the United States, District of Columbia, or U.S. possessions or territories.
agi.p.foreignEarnedIncomeTT2: If you’re a U.S. government employee working outside the United States, that income is not considered foreign earned.
agi.p.foreignEarnedIncomeTT3: If you're not a government employee and you work at an embassy, we consider that income as foreign income for the EITC.
agi.p.foreignEarnedIncomeTT5: Note: If you elect to file Form 2555, you are not eligible to claim the EITC.
agi.p.healthInsuranceTT: Enter any premiums you paid pre-tax for health insurance that you didn't include in the HSA, FSA, or other “cafeteria plan” section.
agi.p.hsaTT1: Enter any Health Savings Account (HSA) contributions that you didn't include under HSA, FSA, or other “cafeteria plan” above.
agi.p.investmentIncomeOver1: You can't claim the earned income credit if your investment income is more than :amount.
agi.p.investmentIncomeOver3: Use Worksheet 1 in :pub596, to figure your investment income.
agi.p.investmentsTT1: Investment income for the EITC includes:
agi.p.investmentsTT3: Use Worksheet 1 in :irsPub596, Earned Income Credit, to figure your investment income if any of the following statements are true for :year:
agi.p.iraTT1: Enter any IRA contributions you made outside of your payroll contributions.
agi.p.mfjIncludeSpouse: Make sure to also include your spouse's income and adjustments.
agi.p.movingTT1: If you are a member of the armed forces on active duty, and the military ordered you to permanently change your duty station, enter any moving expenses the military didn't reimburse you for here. Moving expenses include:
agi.p.noEarnedIncomeWarning1: You must have some earned income to qualify for the EITC.
agi.p.noEarnedIncomeWarning2: For more information about unearned income visit :whatIsEarnedIncome
agi.p.onlyPenalIncomeWarning1: You must have some earned income other than what you earned while an inmate in a penal institution to qualify for the EITC.
agi.p.onlyPenalIncomeWarning2: For more information, see :pub596 Earnings while an inmate.
agi.p.pensionTT1: If you receive retirement benefits in the form of pension or annuity payments from a qualified employer retirement plan, all or some portion of the amounts you receive may be taxable.
agi.p.pensionTT4: Additional rules apply if you received payments before age 59 and a half, are a survivor or beneficiary, or have other situations.
agi.p.previousMessage: On this step you will determine your Adjusted Gross Income (AGI) by entering your pre-tax income, any payroll deductions, and Adjustments to Income. You must answer these questions in order.
agi.p.prisonIncomeTT1: Pay you received for work you performed while an inmate in a penal institution isn't earned income when figuring the earned income credit. This includes paid work performed while in a work release program or while in a halfway house.
agi.p.prisonIncomeTT2: For more information, see :irsPub596Inmate, Earned Income Credit, Earnings while an inmate.
agi.p.scholarshipTT1: Enter any amount of taxable scholarship not reported on your Form W-2. This amount includes scholarship or grant money not found on your W-2 if the money was:
agi.p.scholarshipTT2: Don't include amounts you received for services required by the National Health Service Corps Scholarship Program, the Armed Forces Health Professions Scholarship and Financial Assistance Program, or a comprehensive student work-learning-service program operated by a work college.
agi.p.selfEmploymentExpensesFINWTT: Enter all ordinary and necessary expenses paid or incurred during the taxable year in carrying on any trade or business other than the performance of services as an employee.
agi.p.selfEmploymentExpensesSEGTT: Enter all ordinary and necessary expenses paid or incurred during the taxable year in carrying on any trade or business other than the performance of services as an employee.
agi.p.selfEmploymentGrossTT2: Your gross income includes :all the income you received. We will subtract your expenses later.
agi.p.selfEmploymentInsuranceFINWTT1: Enter the amount you paid for health insurance for yourself, your spouse, and your dependents, if you are self-employed.
agi.p.selfEmploymentInsuranceFINWTT2: For more information see Chapter 6 of :irsPub535.
agi.p.selfEmploymentInsuranceSEGTT1: Enter the amount you paid for health insurance for yourself, your spouse, and your dependents, if you are self-employed.
agi.p.selfEmploymentInsuranceSEGTT2: For more information see Chapter 6 of :irsPub535.
agi.p.selfEmploymentPaidFINWTT: Self-employment taxes refers to the Medicare and Social Security taxes you paid. Divide by 2 the total amount you paid for :year. Enter the result here.
agi.p.selfEmploymentPaidSEGTT: Self-employment taxes refers to the Medicare and Social Security taxes you paid. Divide by 2 the total amount you paid for :year. Enter the result here.
agi.p.sepSimpleFINWTT1: If you are self-employed, you calculate your qualified plan compensation by subtracting the following from your net earnings from self-employment:
agi.p.sepSimpleSEGTT1: If you are self-employed, you calculate your qualified plan compensation by subtracting the following from your net earnings from self-employment:
agi.p.socialSecurityRailroadTT1: For the EITC, include only the taxable amount of your Social Security and railroad retirement benefits. :irsNotice703 can help you determine if any of your Social Security benefits are taxable.
agi.p.socialSecurityRailroadTT3: To compute the taxable amount of your benefits, see Which worksheet to use in :irsPub915.
agi.p.studentLoanTT1: Enter up to $2,500 of the interest you paid on your qualified student loan. You can find this on your Form 1098-E if you paid more than $600 in interest.
agi.p.studentLoanTT2: The loan must have paid qualified higher education expenses to attend an eligible educational institution. Also, the expenses must have been:
agi.p.studentLoanTT3: You can't claim the deduction if you aren't legally obligated to make payments on the loan.
agi.p.taxDeferredRetirementTT1: Enter any pre-tax contributions (payroll deductions) for the following retirement plans:
agi.p.unemploymentInsuranceTT1: For the EITC, include your full unemployment compensation benefits. You find this income on Form 1099-G.
agi.p.unemploymentInsuranceTT2: If your employer's fund provided supplemental benefits, include any amounts reported on Form W-2, :box1.
agi.p.wagesFederalWithheldTT1: Your employer reported your wages, salaries, tips, and other compensation in :box1 of your Form W-2. Include the full amount of tip and overtime income in the calculation of earned income when determining eligibility for the earned income tax credit, even if all or part is deductible.
agi.p.wagesFederalWithheldTT2: :dontInclude your Form W-2 pre-tax contributions in the pre-tax contributions section.
agi.p.wagesFederalWithheldTT2-2: Include all amounts reported in :box1 of Form W-2 for both you and your spouse if you're filing a joint tax return with your spouse.
agi.p.wagesFederalWithheldTT3: You can also choose to include your nontaxable combat pay in earned income for the EITC. Your employer reported your nontaxable combat pay in :box12 with :codeQ on your Form W-2.
agi.p.wagesFederalWithheldTT4: This choice may increase or decrease your EITC. Calculate your EITC both ways to see which option provides the greater benefit. For more information, see Nontaxable combat pay in :irsPub596, Earned Income Credit.
agi.p.wagesFederalWithheldTT5: If you took parental leave or were temporarily disabled during the year and your employer paid you, they reported your income on your W-2. Enter that income here.
agi.p.wagesFederalWithheldTT6: Only the taxable amount of strike benefits paid by a union to its members are earned income.
agi.subheading.boldInstructions: Do not enter the same income in multiple places.
agi.subheading.instructions: Please select and enter your :year income and adjustments. Only select one item per source.
agi.subheading.instructionsMarried: Please select and enter your :year and your spouse's income and adjustments. Only select one item per source.
agi.subheading.married: In this step, we'll estimate your and your spouse's joint earned income, adjusted gross income, and any adjustments to it.
agi.subheading.unMarried: In this step, we'll estimate your earned income, adjusted gross income, and any adjustments to it.

child.p.finalBlockTitleSingle: You have 3 qualifying children and have reached the maximum number of qualifying children that can be claimed for EITC. You don't need to answer any more questions.

children.li.permanentlyDisabledTT1: The child can't perform significant duties over a reasonable period of time while working for pay or profit in a competitive work situation for at least the minimum wage because of a physical or mental condition, and
children.li.permanentlyDisabledTT2: A physician determines the condition has lasted or can be expected to last continuously for at least a year or can lead to death

children.p.claimOtherConfirmTT1: Only one person can claim each child for the EITC. If more than one person claims the child, then the IRS applies tiebreaker rules.
children.p.claimOtherTT1: Only one person can claim each child for the EITC. If more than one person claims the child, then the IRS applies tiebreaker rules.
children.p.claimOtherTT2: For more information, see :irsPub519, Earned Income Credit, Your Qualifying Child Cannot Be Used By More Than One Person.
children.p.fileJointConfirmTT1: You can claim a married person who files a joint return as a qualifying child for the EITC if the child and their spouse file the joint return only to claim a refund of withheld income taxes from their paychecks or estimated taxes they paid. They may not claim any credits such as the EITC on their return.
children.p.fileJointConfirmTT2: For more information see :irsPub519, Earned Income Credit, Joint Return Test.
children.p.fileJointTT1: You generally can't claim a married person as a qualifying child for the EITC if he or she files a joint return.
children.p.fileJointTT2: For more information see :irsPub519, Earned Income Credit, Joint Return Test.
children.p.finalBlockP: You can claim up to three qualifying children for the EITC
children.p.finalMessageHTML: If you have other children or relatives you believe may be qualifying children and were not disqualified in this step, please return to :link to update your number of children.
children.p.introNoQC1: Based on your previous inputs, you don't have any qualifying children. If this isn't correct, please return to :link and change your responses.
children.p.introNoQC2: Please go to the next step to see your results.
children.p.introOneQC: :bold If this isn't correct, please return to :link and change your responses. In this step, we'll see if you may claim this child as a qualifying child for the EITC.
children.p.introOneQCBold: Based on your previous responses, you may have 1 qualifying child with a valid Social Security number.
children.p.introPlus: In this step, we'll see how many of your children or other relatives are your qualifying children for the purposes of claiming and calculating the EITC. You can claim up to three qualifying children for the EITC.
children.p.live51PctTT1: A qualifying child must have the same main home as you for more than half of :year and that home must have been in the United States. The United States includes the 50 states and the District of Columbia. It does not include Puerto Rico or United States territories such as Guam. For the EITC, the IRS considers U.S. military personnel stationed outside the United States on extended active duty to have a main home in the United States during that period of duty.
children.p.live51PctTT2: If the child was born or died in :year, the child must have lived with you for more than half the part of the year he or she was alive.
children.p.live51PctTT3: For the EITC, a temporary absence counts as time lived with you. Examples include:
children.p.live51PctTT4: For more information, see :irsPub519, Earned Income Credit, residency test.
children.p.live51PctTT5: If you adopted the child in :year1, the child was lawfully placed with you for legal adoption by you in :year2, or the child was an eligible foster child placed with you during :year3, your main home must have been this child’s main home for more than half the time since he or she was adopted or placed with you in :year4.
children.p.noQCAlert: The child must have the same main home as you in the United States for more than half the year. See :link, Earned Income Credit, for the residency test.
children.p.noQcButCreditMessage: If you have other children or relatives you believe may qualify, please add them on this page. You can claim up to three qualifying children for the EITC.
children.p.permanentlyDisabledTT1: For the EITC, we consider a child permanently and totally disabled if both of the following are true:
children.p.permanentlyDisabledTT2: For more information see :irsPub519, Earned Income Credit, Permanently and Totally Disabled.
children.p.previous: On this step you will determine which of your children or other relatives are qualifying children for the purposes of claiming and calculating the Earned Income Tax Credit. You must answer these questions in order.
children.p.relationshipTT1: A step relationship is one formed by a marriage. For the EITC, a relationship formed by a marriage, such as a stepdaughter, stepson, stepbrother, or stepsister, doesn't end if the marriage ends.
children.p.relationshipTT2: An adopted child is always treated as your own child. This term includes a child who was lawfully placed with you for legal adoption.
children.p.relationshipTT3: You can claim a foster child only if the child is placed with you by an authorized placement agency or by court order.
children.p.relationshipTT5: This term includes a state or local government agency or an Indian tribal government. It also includes a tax-exempt organization licensed by a state or an Indian tribal government.
children.p.showNoCreditMessage: Your AGI is above the limit for the number of potential Qualifying Children left, you do not qualify for EITC.
children.p.showNotLiveInUS50Message: You must live in the U.S 50% of the year to qualify for EITC.
children.p.studentTT1: To be considered full-time, the student must have enrolled for the number of hours or courses the school considers to be full-time attendance.
children.p.studentTT2: Students who work on "co-op" jobs in private industry as a part of a school's regular course of classroom and practical training are considered full-time students.
children.p.studentTT3: For the EITC, a school is:
children.p.us50PercentTT1: Your home must have been in the United States for more than half of :year. The United States includes the 50 states and the District of Columbia. It does not include Unites States possessions such as Guam, the Virgin Islands, or Puerto Rico.
children.p.us50PercentTT2: If you're a member of the United States military and you were stationed outside the United States on extended active duty, select :yes
children.p.youngerSpouseTT1: To be a qualifying child, the child must be younger than you or your spouse unless the child is permanently and totally disabled at any time during :year.
children.p.youngerTT1: To be a qualifying child, the child must be younger than you unless the child is permanently and totally disabled at any time during :year.
children.title.noQCAlert: You can't claim this child for the EITC

filingStatus.h3.fsToolResultTitle: Based on your choices, your filing status is :fsCalc.
filingStatus.label.houseUpkeep3Dependent: Where you lived more than half the year with a dependent (such as a child, grandchild, sibling, niece, nephew, parent or grandparent)
filingStatus.label.houseUpkeep3Parent: For a parent you can claim as a dependent, but who didn't live with you
filingStatus.label.houseUpkeep3SpousesCh: Where you lived for more than half the year with a child who isn't your dependent even though you're the custodial parent (because of the rule for children of divorced or separated parents)
filingStatus.legend.claimDependent1: Can you claim your child or stepchild who lived with you as a dependent for :year?
filingStatus.legend.claimDependent2: Could you claim your child or stepchild as a dependent EXCEPT that, for :year:
filingStatus.legend.claimDependent2Li1: The child had gross income that was above the threshold of :limit for :year,
filingStatus.legend.claimDependent2Li2: The child filed a joint return, or
filingStatus.legend.claimDependent2Li3: You could be claimed as a dependent on someone else's return?
filingStatus.legend.deceasedSpouseAge: Please select the age that best describes the age your spouse was when they died:
filingStatus.legend.deceasedSpouseValidS: Did your spouse have a valid Social Security number on the day they passed away?
filingStatus.legend.entitledJointReturnD: Were you entitled to file a joint return with your spouse for the year your spouse died?
filingStatus.legend.fileJointReturn: Do you and your spouse intend to file joint returns?
filingStatus.legend.filingStatus: Please select the filing status you will use when you file your :year taxes:
filingStatus.legend.fsKnown: Do you know the filing status you will use when filing your :year taxes?
filingStatus.legend.houseUpkeep1: In :year1, did you pay more than half the cost of keeping up a home in which you lived that was also the main home of your dependent child, stepchild, or foster child for all of :year2 (including any temporary absences)?
filingStatus.legend.houseUpkeep2: In :year1, did you pay more than half the cost of keeping up a home in which you lived that was also the main home of your child or stepchild for all of :year2 (including any temporary absences)?
filingStatus.legend.houseUpkeep3: Did you pay more than 50% of the cost of keeping up a home?
filingStatus.legend.intendJointReturnDec: Do you intend to file a joint return with your deceased spouse for :year?
filingStatus.legend.liveWithSpouse: Did your spouse live with you in your home at any time during the last six months of :year (including any temporary absences)?
filingStatus.legend.maritalStatus: What was your marital status on December 31, :year?
filingStatus.legend.claimChildren: Do you plan to claim children or other relatives with valid Social Security numbers for the EITC?
filingStatus.legend.spouseAge: Please select the age bracket that best describes your spouse's age at the end of :year.
filingStatus.legend.spouseValidSSN: Does your spouse have a valid Social Security number that was issued on or before the due date of your :year tax return (including extensions)?
filingStatus.legend.spousesPassing: When did your spouse pass away?
filingStatus.legend.us50Percent: Was your main home in the U.S. for more than half of :year?
filingStatus.legend.us50PercentMarried: Was your and your spouse's main home in the U.S. for more than half of :year?
filingStatus.legend.wasSpouseAliveEndOfY: Was your spouse alive on December 31, :year?
filingStatus.li.claimDependentTT3: Or other relatives who live with you and whose care you are responsible for.
filingStatus.li.filingStatusTT1: You may claim Head of Household filing status only if you’re not married at the end of the year or you lived apart from your spouse for the last six months of the year.
filingStatus.li.filingStatusTT2: You must also have paid more than 50% of the costs of keeping up the home in which you lived with certain relatives or the home of your mother or father.
filingStatus.li.filingStatusTT3: You could have filed a joint return with your spouse for the year your spouse died. It does not matter whether you filed a joint return.
filingStatus.li.filingStatusTT4: Your spouse died in one of the two years before :year1 and you did not remarry before the end of :year2.
filingStatus.li.filingStatusTT5: You paid more than half the cost of keeping up a home for the year.
filingStatus.li.filingStatusTT6: You have a child or stepchild whom you can claim as a dependent. This does not include a foster child.
filingStatus.li.filingStatusTT7: This child lived in your home all year, except for temporary absences. :note There are exceptions for a child who was born, died, adopted, or placed into foster care during the year and for a kidnapped child.
filingStatus.li.filingStatusTT8: Your spouse died in :year1 or :year2 and you did not remarry before the end of :year3.
filingStatus.li.filingStatusTT9: This child lived in your home all year, except for temporary absences. :note There are exceptions for a child who was born or died during the year and for a kidnapped child.
filingStatus.li.houseUpkeepTT1: Rent, mortgage interest, real estate taxes, and home insurance
filingStatus.li.houseUpkeepTT6: Medical treatment, medical insurance payments, and prescription drugs
filingStatus.li.houseUpkeepTT8: Transportation costs like insurance, lease payments, or public transportation
filingStatus.li.houseUpkeepTT10: Value of your services or those of a member of your household
filingStatus.li.noDependentsAgeWarning1: You or your spouse must be between 25-64, or
filingStatus.li.noDependentsAgeWarning2: You must have at least one qualifying child
qualifyingChildren.li.validSSNTT3: Social Security number issued solely to allow the recipient of the SSN to apply for or receive a federally funded benefit such as Medicaid
qualifyingChildren.li.validSSNTT4: Social Security number issued solely to apply for or receive a federally funded benefit such as Medicaid
filingStatus.li.primarySupportTT5: Rent paid (or fair rental value of the home)
filingStatus.li.spouseValidSSNTT3: Social Security number issued after the due date of the return, including extensions
filingStatus.li.spouseValidSSNTT4: Social Security number issued solely to allow the recipient of the SSN to apply for or receive a federally funded benefit such as Medicaid
filingStatus.p.claimDependentTT1: A step relationship is one formed by a marriage. For the EITC, a relationship formed by a marriage, such as a stepdaughter, stepson, stepbrother, or stepsister, doesn't end if the marriage ends.
filingStatus.p.claimDependentTT2: A dependent is usually, but not always, a child or other relative who meets a set of criteria. A dependent must be a U.S. citizen, U.S. national or resident alien. Most dependents include:
filingStatus.p.claimDependentTT3: For more information about whether you can claim someone as a dependent, visit :whoMayIClaim
filingStatus.p.deceasedSpouseValidSSNWar: You can't claim the EITC if you choose to file a joint return with your deceased spouse because your spouse did not have a valid social security number on the day they passed away.
filingStatus.p.fileJointTT: You can choose to file one return with your spouse by selecting :mfj as your filing status. This allows you to combine your income and expenses when figuring your credits, deductions, and tax liability.
filingStatus.p.filingStatusTT1: You may claim Head of Household filing status only if you’re not married at the end of the year or you lived apart from your spouse for the last six months of the year. You must also have paid more than 50% of the costs of keeping up the home in which you lived with certain relatives or the home of your mother or father.
filingStatus.p.filingStatusTT3: To file as a qualifying surviving spouse all the following must apply to you:
filingStatus.p.filingStatusTT4: For tax years before 2022, a Qualifying Surviving Spouse was known as a Qualifying Surviving Spouse. You may choose this filing status if you continue to meet these requirements for Tax Year 2022.
filingStatus.p.filingStatusTT5: To file as a qualifying widow(er) all the following must apply to you:
filingStatus.p.houseUpkeepTT1: If the total amount you paid is more than half the total cost, you meet the requirement of paying more than half the cost of keeping up the home.
filingStatus.p.houseUpkeepTTN: :note Costs paid with public assistance are consider costs paid by the taxpayer.
filingStatus.p.intendJointReturnDeceased: If your spouse died during :year, you can choose to file one return with your spouse by selecting :mfj as your filing status. This allows you to combine your income and expenses when figuring your credits, deductions, and tax liability.
filingStatus.p.liveWithSpouseTT1: A person is considered to have lived with you during periods of time when one or both of you are temporarily absent due to special circumstances such as:
filingStatus.p.liveWithSpouseTT2: It must be reasonable to assume that the absent person will return to the home after the temporary absence.
filingStatus.p.maritalStatusTT1: "Legally separated" for this purpose means legally separated under a decree of divorce or separate maintenance.
filingStatus.p.maritalStatusTT2: Some states allow common law marriages. Check with your state to see if you are considered married under common law.
filingStatus.p.mfsWarning1: If you file as married filing separately, you do not qualify for the Earned Income Tax Credit.
filingStatus.p.mfsWarning2: If you have questions about your filing status, see :irsPub501.
filingStatus.p.mfsWarning3: See :irsPub596, for information on who qualifies for the EITC.
filingStatus.p.noDependentsAgeWarning: You do not qualify for EITC for one of the following reasons:
qualifyingChildren.p.validSSNTT1: The amount of your EITC depends on the number of qualifying children you have with valid SSNs. Each child or relative you claim for purposes of calculating the amount of the EITC must have a valid Social Security number (SSN) that is issued by the Social Security Administration on or before the due date of your tax return (including extensions).
qualifyingChildren.p.validSSNTT2: For the EITC, an SSN is a valid SSN unless “Not Valid for Employment” is printed on the Social Security card and the number was issued solely to allow the recipient of the SSN to apply for or receive a federally funded benefit. An example of a federally funded benefit is Medicaid.
qualifyingChildren.p.validSSNTT3: We accept a Social Security card with "Valid for work with DHS authorization" on it only as long as the DHS authorization is still valid.
qualifyingChildren.p.validSSNTT4: For the EITC, we don't accept:
filingStatus.p.primarySupportTT1: You provided primary support if you paid more than half of the person's expenses during the year.
filingStatus.p.primarySupportTT3: Include this person's portion of shared expenses, such as:
filingStatus.p.skipAhead: On this step you will be asked about how you plan to file your :year taxes. You must answer these questions in order.
filingStatus.p.skipAheadNoYear: On this step you will be asked about how you plan to file your taxes. You must answer these questions in order.
filingStatus.p.spouseValidSSNTT1: To claim EITC with a qualifying  child, the child must have a valid SSN issued by the Social Security Administration on or before the due date of your :year return (including extensions).
filingStatus.p.spouseValidSSNTT2: If the Social Security card reads "Valid for work with DHS authorization" or “Valid for work with INS authorization” on it, it is a valid SSN if the  authorization is still valid.
filingStatus.p.spouseValidSSNTT3: If your Social Security card reads "Valid for work with DHS authorization" or "Valid for work with INS authorization" on it only as long as the DHS authorization is still valid.
filingStatus.p.spouseValidSSNTT4: For the EITC, we don't accept:
filingStatus.p.spouseValidSSNTT5: See :irsPub596 for more information on a :validSSN.
filingStatus.p.spouseValidSSNWarning: You can't claim the EITC because your spouse doesn't have a valid Social Security number issued on or before the due date of your tax return.
filingStatus.p.subtitle: On this step you will be asked about how you plan to file your :year taxes.
filingStatus.p.us50PercentTT1: Your home must have been in the United States for more than half of :year. The United States includes the 50 states and the District of Columbia. It does not include Unites States possessions such as Guam, the Virgin Islands, or Puerto Rico.
filingStatus.p.us50PercentTT2: If you're a member of the United States military and you were stationed outside the United States on extended active duty, select :yes.
filingStatus.p.us50PercentWarning: To claim the EITC, your home must have been in the United States for more than half the year. The United States includes the 50 states and the District of Columbia.

generalInfo.h2.title: General Information
generalInfo.p.subtitle: Answer a few quick questions about yourself to see if you qualify.
generalInfo.legend.age: Please select the range that describes your age at the end of :year.
generalInfo.legend.citizen: Were you a U.S. Citizen or Resident Alien for all of :year?
generalInfo.legend.claimedAsDependent: Will someone else claim you as a dependent or qualifying child for the EITC on their tax return?
generalInfo.legend.foreignIncome: Are you filing Form 2555, Foreign Earned Income?
generalInfo.legend.validSSN: Do you have a valid Social Security number that was issued on or before the due date of your :year tax return (including extensions)?
generalInfo.legend.year: Select the tax year you would like to check your EITC eligibility for:
generalInfo.li.claimedAsDependentTT1: In most cases, if you're under age 19 or a student under age 24 and live with your parents or other relatives in the United States for more than half of the year, select :yesBold.
generalInfo.li.claimedAsDependentTT2: If you’re married and filing a joint return, you can’t be claimed as a dependent unless you’re filing a joint return :only  to claim a refund of withheld income taxes or estimated taxes paid.
generalInfo.li.validSSNTT3: Social Security number issued after the due date of the return, including extensions
generalInfo.li.validSSNTT4: Social Security number issued solely to apply for or receive a federally funded benefit such as Medicaid
generalInfo.p.citizenTT1: If you are not a U.S. citizen, you're considered an alien.
generalInfo.p.citizenTT2: You're considered a resident alien for the EITC if you have a green card, or you lived in the U.S. for more than a certain number of days.
generalInfo.p.citizenTT3: For more information and important exceptions, see :irsPub519, U.S. Tax Guide for Aliens.
generalInfo.p.citizenWarning1: You must be a U.S. citizen or resident alien to claim the Earned Income Tax Credit. See :irsPub519, U.S. Tax Guide for Aliens, to determine if you are a U.S. resident alien for the entire year.
generalInfo.p.citizenWarning2: :note Select :yes if you are filing a joint return with your spouse for :year and either you or your spouse were a U.S. Citizen or Resident alien for the entire year.
generalInfo.p.claimedAsDependentTT1: In most cases you cannot be a dependent or qualifying child of another taxpayer and claim the EITC.
generalInfo.p.claimedAsDependentTT2: For more information about whether someone can claim you as a dependent, visit :whoMayIClaim
generalInfo.p.claimedAsDependentTT3: For more information about whether someone can claim you as a qualifying child for the EITC, see :irsPub596, Earned Income Credit, You Cannot Be a Qualifying Child of Another Taxpayer.
generalInfo.p.claimedAsDependentTT4: We'll ask about children or relatives you might claim later.
generalInfo.p.claimedAsDependentWarning1: You don't qualify for the EITC if you are someone else's qualifying child for the EITC.
generalInfo.p.claimedAsDependentWarning2: For more information about whether someone can claim you as a qualifying child for the EITC, see :link, Earned Income Credit, You Cannot Be a Qualifying Child of Another Taxpayer.
generalInfo.p.claimedAsDependentWarning3: You don't qualify for the EITC if you are someone else's dependent unless you have a qualifying child for the EITC.
generalInfo.p.foreignIncomeTT: This refers to income you earned outside the United States, District of Columbia, or U.S. possessions or territories which you have elected to exclude on a :pub2555
generalInfo.p.foreignIncomeWarning1: You can't claim the earned income credit if you file :form2555
generalInfo.p.validSSNTT1: You must have a valid Social Security number (SSN) that is issued by the Social Security Administration on or before the due date of your :year tax return (including extensions).
generalInfo.p.validSSNTT2: If your Social Security card reads “Valid for work with DHS authorization” or “Valid for work with INS authorization” on it, it is a valid SSN if the authorization is still valid.
generalInfo.p.validSSNTT4: For the EITC, we don't accept:
generalInfo.p.validSSNTT5: See :irsPub596 for more information on a :validSocial.
generalInfo.p.validSSNWarning: You can't claim the EITC because you don't have a valid Social Security number issued on or before the due date of your tax return.
generalInfo.p.yearTT: For wages and other income earned in:
generalInfo.span.validSSNError: Valid Social Security Number is required
global.heading.eitcNoQualify: You don't qualify for the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC).
global.p.appTitle: The Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC) helps low-to-moderate income workers and families get a tax break. Answer some questions to see if you qualify.
global.p.usGov: An official website of the United States government
global.span.asterisk: All fields marked with an asterisk :* are required.
global.span.error: You must enter an amount for :label
qualifyingChildren.p.removeChildParagrap: Are you sure you want to remove this child from your EITC calculation?
qualifyingChildren.h2.maxChildernHeader: Editing not allowed since you have reached the maximum number of qualifying children
qualifyingChildren.p.maxChildernParagrap: Based on your inputs, you have 3 qualifying children with valid Social Security numbers and have reached the maximum number that be claimed for EITC.
You must remove a qualifying child before you can edit this one.
qualifyingChildren.h2.incompleteChildHea: You have not completed adding a child. What would you like to do?
qualifyingChildren.p.incompleteChildPara: You may go to the next step, but you will lose any information you have entered for this child.
Click 'CANCEL' below to go back and complete the process of adding this child.

results.h2.heading: Your Results
results.h2.nextSteps: Next Steps: How to file for the EITC
results.p.amended: If you already filed your taxes without claiming the EITC and are eligible, you can file an amended return to claim the EITC.
results.p.importantNote: :bold The accuracy of this estimate depends on the accuracy of the information you provide.  The IRS makes no guarantees about the accuracy of this estimate and accepts no liability resulting from your use of the estimation.
results.p.introTop: Based on the information you provided, you are eligible for the Earned Income Tax Credit estimated amount of:
results.p.introTop2: This credit amount will reduce your :year taxes when you file your tax return with the IRS. If the credit reduces your tax to less than zero, you may get a refund.
results.p.online: If you'd like to file your taxes online, you qualify to use the IRS Free File Program. Please note the program is only available from January through October.
results.p.paperOneLinkNoQC: Since you don't have a qualifying child, you just have to include the amount of the EIC you qualify for on your :link1.
results.p.paperThreeLinks: Since you have a qualifying child, you must fill out :link0 and attach it to your :link1 or :link2. You may also search for additional forms on IRS.gov.
results.p.paperTwoLinkNoQC: Since you don't have a qualifying child, you just have to include the amount of the EIC you qualify for on your :link1 or :link2.
results.p.paperTwoLinks: Since you have a qualifying child, you must fill out a :link0 and attach it to your :link1. You may also search for additional forms on IRS.gov.
results.p.person: If you'd prefer to work with an in-person tax preparer, you have the option for doing this at an IRS Volunteer Income Tax Assistance (VITA) clinic.
results.p.previousMessage: Information displayed on this page is dependent upon your answers in previous steps. Please answer all previous steps before advancing to this page.
results.p.secondIntro: In order to receive the estimated amount of :amount*, you must file your :year1 federal tax return. If you claim the EITC, by law the IRS cannot issue your tax refund before mid-February :year2, even the portion that is not associated with the EITC.
results.p.summary: The table below is a summary of the information you entered in the tool. If any of this information is incorrect please click edit to update it, otherwise, it could affect your results.
filingStatus.legend.principalRes: Did you and your spouse have the same principal residence for the last 6 months of :year?
filingStatus.legend.mfsLegalDoc: Were you legally separated according to your state law under a written separation agreement, or a decree of separate maintenance and you did not live with your spouse on the last day of :year?
filingStatus.legend.mfsLegalDoc2: Did you live apart from your spouse at the end of 2021 AND were separated according to your state law under a written separation agreement or a decree of separate maintenance?
filingStatus.legend.spouseStudent: Was your spouse a specified student in :year?
filingStatus.legend.deceasedSpouseStuden: Was your spouse a specified student in :year?
filingStatus.p.spouseStudentTT1: A specified student is an individual who during :year carried at least half the normal full-time workload at a higher education institution, for a period of 5 months or greater, and who is not a qualified former foster youth or a qualified homeless youth.
agi.p.priorYearIncome: If your earned income was higher in 2019 than in 2021 or 2020, you can use the 2019 amount to figure your EITC for 2021 or 2020.
filingStatus.p.mfsLegalDocWarning1: You can claim the EIC if you are married, not filing a joint return, had a qualifying child who lived with you for more than half of :year, and either of the following apply.
filingStatus.li.mfsLegalDocWarning2: You lived apart from your spouse for the last 6 months of :year1, or
filingStatus.li.mfsLegalDocWarning3: You are legally separated according to your state law under a written separation agreement, or a decree of separate maintenance and you didn't live in the same household as your spouse at the end of :year2.
generalInfo.legend.student: Were you a specified student in :year?
generalInfo.p.studentTT1: A specified student is an individual who during :year carried at least half the normal full-time workload at a higher education institution, for a period of 5 months or greater, and who is not a qualified former foster youth or a qualified homeless youth.

filingStatus.p.noDependentsAgeWarningFS: You can't claim the EITC  if you are married and not filing a joint return with your spouse unless you lived with a qualifying child for more than half the year and either lived apart from your spouse for the last six months of :year1 or were  legally separated according to your state law under a written separation agreement or a decree of separate maintenance and lived apart from your spouse at the end of :year2.
filingStatus.p.noDependentsAgeWarning3: You don't qualify for EITC without a qualifying child unless you, or your spouse if filing a joint return, are between age 25 and 64.
filingStatus.p.noDependentsAgeWarning4: You don't qualify for EITC without a qualifying child unless you, or your spouse if filing a joint return, are one of the following ages:
filingStatus.li.noDependentsAgeWarning4: At least age 19 but not a specified student in :year, or
filingStatus.li.noDependentsAgeWarning5: At least age 18 and a qualified former foster youth or qualified homeless youth in :year.
